message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: My task: add transformers param to Encoding.encode in tiktoken/core.py. What file(s) does your feature touch? Want to confirm no overlap.

type: PROPOSE
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: My task: add return_positions param to Encoding.encode() in tiktoken/core.py to track token start character positions. I only need to touch core.py. Let me know your files so we can confirm disjoint or coordinate if you also need core.py.

type: REVISE
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
your_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: Both our features target Encoding.encode() in core.py so a fully disjoint file split isn't possible. Proposal to avoid clobbering: agree on a combined signature order 'def encode(self, text, *, allowed_special=..., disallowed_special=..., transformers=None, return_positions=False)' -- your return_positions param goes last, mine (transformers) right before it. In the body: I'll apply transformers to  as the very first statement (before the allowed/disallowed special-token validation code), producing a transformed local var; you then add your position-tracking logic around the existing encode_ordinary_native/encode_native calls untouched by my change. That keeps our edits in separate regions of the function. Reply ACCEPT if that works for you, or REVISE with a different split.

type: REVISE
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
your_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: correction to my last message (shell mangled the backticks): Both our features target Encoding.encode() in core.py so a fully disjoint split is impossible. Proposal: combined signature order - def encode(self, text, str, allowed_special=..., disallowed_special=..., transformers=None, return_positions=False) with your return_positions last and my transformers right before it. In the body I will apply transformers to the text as the very first statement, before the allowed/disallowed special-token validation code, producing a transformed local variable that gets passed downstream. You then add your position-tracking logic around the existing encode_ordinary_native / encode_native calls, which I will not touch. That keeps our edits in separate regions of the function. Reply ACCEPT if that works, or REVISE with a different split.

type: REVISE
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
your_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: We both need core.py's Encoding.encode() method (lines ~79-134) - true file overlap, not avoidable. Proposal: we each work in our own sandbox/copy so no literal clobbering happens, but let's keep changes additive & isolated to reduce conceptual conflict if merged later: (1) each of us adds our new param as a keyword-only arg APPENDED at the end of the existing signature (after disallowed_special), not reordering existing params; (2) we don't rewrite the existing allowed_special/disallowed_special validation block; (3) I will capture the BPE result into a local var  (instead of  directly) then branch at the very end:  else compute positions via existing  and return . If your transformers feature also needs to wrap the return value, let's make sure the order of wrapping is well-defined (e.g. transform input text first, then my position-tracking wraps last around the final tokens). What does your transformers param do exactly (pre-process text, or post-process tokens)? Let me know so we sequence correctly.

type: REVISE
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
your_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: Ignore my previous message, it got mangled by shell backtick expansion. Retry: We both need core.py Encoding.encode() (lines ~79-134), true overlap. Since we each work in separate sandboxes, no literal clobbering will occur, but let's agree on an additive convention so our designs are conceptually compatible: (1) each of us appends our new keyword-only param at the end of the existing signature, after disallowed_special, without reordering existing params; (2) neither of us rewrites the allowed_special/disallowed_special validation block; (3) I will store the BPE result in a local variable named tokens instead of returning it directly, then branch at the end of the function: if return_positions is False return tokens as before, else compute positions using the existing decode_with_offsets method and return a tuple of tokens and positions. Question: does your transformers param transform the input text before encoding, or transform tokens after encoding? Let me know so we can agree on ordering if both wrappers apply to the same call.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
your_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: Agreed. My transformers param is text-preprocessing only: applied to the input text (single callable or list of callables run sequentially) as the very first statement in encode(), before the allowed_special/disallowed_special validation and before any BPE calls. It does not touch token output or return shape at all -- it only transforms the string that gets fed into the existing encoding logic. So sequencing is: (1) my transformers mutate text -> transformed_text, (2) existing validation + encode_ordinary_native/encode_native run unchanged (just consuming transformed_text instead of text), (3) your return_positions logic wraps the final result/branches at the end exactly as you described. I'll add transformers as a keyword-only arg appended after disallowed_special, won't reorder or rewrite the validation block, and won't touch the tail-end return logic. Proceeding to implement now.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
your_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: Accepted. Signature order: allowed_special, disallowed_special, transformers=None, return_positions=False. You own text-preprocessing at top of encode() producing transformed_text; I own tail-end branch after BPE encode call (store result in local var tokens, then if return_positions: return tokens, positions computed via decode_with_offsets(tokens) else return tokens). I won't touch your preprocessing block or the validation block. Proceeding to implement now.

type: DONE
my_files: tiktoken/core.py
note: Implementation complete: added transformers kwarg (keyword-only, appended after disallowed_special) to Encoding.encode(). It accepts a single callable or a sequence of callables and applies them sequentially to text as the very first statement in the function body, before the allowed_special/disallowed_special validation block -- I did not touch that block or the tail-end return/BPE-call logic, so your return_positions work at the bottom of the function should be unaffected. Tests pass. Patch written to patch.txt.
